content.body:
NUTRITION is the process by which organisms obtain and use food for energy, growth and repair.
AUTOTROPHS (e.g. green plants) make their own food using light energy — PHOTOSYNTHESIS.
HETEROTROPHS (e.g. animals, fungi) obtain food by eating other organisms.

Photosynthesis equation:
6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
(carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen)

CONDITIONS NEEDED FOR PHOTOSYNTHESIS:
• Light (energy source) — absorbed by chlorophyll in chloroplasts
• Water (from soil via roots and xylem)
• Carbon dioxide (from air through stomata)
• Chlorophyll (green pigment in chloroplasts)

MINERAL SALTS for plants (from soil):
• Nitrates (NO₃⁻) → protein and DNA synthesis
• Phosphates (PO₄³⁻) → ATP and DNA
• Magnesium (Mg²⁺) → part of chlorophyll
• Calcium (Ca²⁺) → cell walls
Deficiency: nitrate → yellowing (chlorosis), stunted growth

content.title: Starch Test on Leaf
content.body:
To test if photosynthesis has occurred:
1. Boil leaf in water (kill cells, stop reactions)
2. Boil in ethanol (remove chlorophyll — leaf turns yellow/white)
3. Rinse in cold water
4. Add iodine solution
Result: Blue-black = starch present (photosynthesis occurred)

questions.question: Photosynthesis takes place in:
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and the machinery for photosynthesis. They are found mainly in leaf cells.

questions.question: The green pigment that absorbs light for photosynthesis is:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs mainly red and blue light (reflects green — hence leaves look green).

questions.question: Which gas is a RAW MATERIAL for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: CO₂ enters through stomata and is used to build glucose in photosynthesis.

questions.question: Water enters the leaf for photosynthesis via:
questions.explanation: Water: roots → xylem vessels (stem) → xylem in leaf → mesophyll cells → chloroplasts.

questions.question: A plant with nitrate deficiency shows:
questions.explanation: Nitrates are needed for protein/chlorophyll synthesis. Deficiency → less chlorophyll → yellowing (chlorosis) + poor growth.

questions.question: The product of photosynthesis used as energy source is:
questions.explanation: Glucose is the direct product of photosynthesis. It is used in respiration or converted to starch for storage.

questions.question: Variegated (green and white) leaves only show starch in:
questions.explanation: Only cells with chlorophyll can photosynthesize. White areas have no chlorophyll → no photosynthesis → no starch.

questions.question: Magnesium deficiency in plants causes:
questions.explanation: Magnesium is the central atom in chlorophyll. Without Mg, no chlorophyll → leaves yellow.

questions.question: Autotrophs are organisms that:
options: Make their own food using light or chemical energy
questions.answer: Make their own food using light or chemical energy
questions.explanation: Auto = self. Autotrophs (plants, algae, some bacteria) synthesise organic food from inorganic molecules.

questions.question: In the starch test, ethanol is used to:
options: Remove chlorophyll so iodine colour is visible
questions.answer: Remove chlorophyll so iodine colour is visible
questions.explanation: Hot ethanol extracts the green chlorophyll, turning leaf yellow-white so the iodine blue-black colour is clearly visible.

questions.question: Oxygen produced in photosynthesis comes from the splitting of:
questions.explanation: In the light reactions, water is split (photolysis): 2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂. O₂ is a byproduct.

questions.question: Rate of photosynthesis increases with:
options: Increasing CO₂ concentration (up to a point)
questions.answer: Increasing CO₂ concentration (up to a point)
questions.explanation: CO₂ is a limiting factor. More CO₂ → more substrate for photosynthesis → faster rate, until another factor limits.

questions.question: Plants store excess glucose as:
questions.explanation: Plants convert glucose to starch (insoluble) for storage in leaves, seeds, tubers. Animals store as glycogen.

questions.question: A leaf destarched by keeping in dark for 48 hours is used to ensure:
options: Any starch found must have been made in the experiment
questions.answer: Any starch found must have been made in the experiment
questions.explanation: Destarching removes pre-existing starch. Any starch found afterward was definitely produced during the experiment.

questions.question: Phosphates from soil are needed by plants for:
questions.explanation: Phosphate groups are essential components of ATP (energy), DNA (genetic material), and phospholipid membranes.

questions.question: The compensation point is when:
options: Rate of photosynthesis equals rate of respiration
CO₂ equals O₂ in the air
questions.answer: Rate of photosynthesis equals rate of respiration
questions.explanation: At the compensation point, O₂ produced by photosynthesis = O₂ consumed by respiration. Net gas exchange = zero.

questions.question: Carbon dioxide enters leaves mainly through:
questions.explanation: Stomata are the main entry points for CO₂ into the leaf. Guard cells control stomatal opening.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT needed for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: O₂ is a PRODUCT of photosynthesis, not a raw material. Raw materials are CO₂ and water; energy is from light.

questions.question: A plant in bright light absorbs more CO₂ than it releases. This means:
questions.explanation: In bright light, photosynthesis is faster than respiration → net absorption of CO₂ and release of O₂.

questions.question: Heterotrophs differ from autotrophs because heterotrophs:
options: Must obtain organic food by eating other organisms
questions.answer: Must obtain organic food by eating other organisms
questions.explanation: Hetero = other. Heterotrophs (animals, fungi, most bacteria) cannot make their own food — they ingest or absorb it.

content.body: Animals are heterotrophs — they cannot make their own food. They must eat other organisms for energy and nutrients. A BALANCED DIET contains all necessary nutrients in the right proportions.

SEVEN FOOD GROUPS:
1. Carbohydrates — energy (rice, maize, bread)
2. Proteins — growth, repair (meat, beans, eggs)
3. Lipids (fats/oils) — energy, insulation, vitamins
4. Vitamins — protect health, enzyme cofactors
5. Minerals — body functions (Ca for bones, Fe for blood)
6. Water — all metabolic reactions, transport
7. Fibre (roughage) — prevents constipation, keeps gut healthy

KEY VITAMINS:
Vitamin A — vision in dim light (deficiency: night blindness)
Vitamin C — immune system, wound healing (deficiency: scurvy)
Vitamin D — calcium absorption for bones (deficiency: rickets)
Vitamin B₁₂ — red blood cell formation (deficiency: anaemia)

MALNUTRITION: Not getting enough nutrients.
Kwashiorkor — protein deficiency (swollen belly)
Marasmus — overall calorie deficiency (severe wasting)
Obesity — excess calorie intake

questions.question: Which food group provides the body's main energy source?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates (glucose) are the body's primary and preferred energy fuel for cellular respiration.

questions.question: Proteins are needed mainly for:
questions.explanation: Proteins are used to build and repair cells, make enzymes, hormones, and antibodies.

questions.question: Vitamin C deficiency causes:
questions.explanation: Vitamin C is needed for collagen synthesis. Deficiency → scurvy: bleeding gums, slow wound healing, fatigue.

questions.question: Calcium mineral is important for:
questions.explanation: Calcium is the main mineral in hydroxyapatite — the crystal structure of bones and tooth enamel.

questions.question: Kwashiorkor results from deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Kwashiorkor: protein deficiency despite adequate calories. Causes oedema (swollen belly), thin limbs, skin lesions.

questions.question: Dietary fibre (roughage) is important because it:
questions.explanation: Insoluble fibre adds bulk to faeces and stimulates gut wall movement (peristalsis), preventing constipation.

questions.question: Vitamin D deficiency in children causes:
questions.explanation: Vitamin D enables calcium absorption. Deficiency → weak bones that bow under weight = rickets in children.

questions.question: Iron in the diet is needed to make:
questions.explanation: Iron is the central atom in haem (part of haemoglobin). Iron deficiency → fewer RBCs → anaemia.

questions.question: Marasmus is caused by:
questions.explanation: Marasmus = severe under-nutrition (starvation). Body wastes away as it breaks down muscle and fat for energy.

questions.question: Which vitamin improves vision in dim light?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A is needed to make rhodopsin (visual purple) in rod cells. Deficiency → night blindness.

questions.question: Water makes up approximately what percentage of the human body?
questions.explanation: About 60-70% of body mass is water. It is the medium for all metabolic reactions.

questions.question: A food test using biuret reagent tests for:
questions.explanation: Biuret reagent (NaOH + CuSO₄) turns purple/violet in the presence of proteins (peptide bonds).

questions.question: Lipids (fats and oils) in the diet are important for:
options: Energy storage, insulation, absorption of fat-soluble vitamins
questions.answer: Energy storage, insulation, absorption of fat-soluble vitamins
questions.explanation: Fats store twice as much energy per gram as carbohydrates. Needed for vitamins A, D, E, K absorption and insulation.

questions.question: Emulsion test detects:
questions.explanation: Emulsion test: dissolve food in ethanol → add water → milky white emulsion = lipid present.

questions.question: Which mineral is needed for thyroid hormone production?
questions.explanation: Iodine is needed to make thyroxine (thyroid hormone). Deficiency → goitre (swollen thyroid), hypothyroidism.

questions.question: A balanced diet must be matched to:
options: Age, sex, activity level, and health status
questions.answer: Age, sex, activity level, and health status
questions.explanation: Nutritional needs differ: athletes need more protein/carbs, pregnant women need more iron/folic acid, growing teens need more calcium.

questions.question: Iodine test for starch: positive result is:
questions.explanation: Iodine solution (orange-brown) + starch → intense blue-black colour as iodine molecules enter the starch helix.

questions.question: Obesity increases risk of:
options: Heart disease, type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure
questions.answer: Heart disease, type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure
questions.explanation: Excess body fat, especially visceral (abdominal) fat, increases risk of metabolic and cardiovascular diseases.

questions.question: Which food is richest in protein?
questions.explanation: Meat, fish, eggs, and legumes (beans, peas) are high-protein foods. Plants (rice, cassava) are mainly carbohydrate.

questions.question: Folic acid (Vitamin B9) is especially important for:
questions.explanation: Folic acid is critical in early pregnancy for neural tube (brain/spine) development. Deficiency → spina bifida.
